text: Pre-running stretching (static, dynamic, or PNF) does not significantly impair running economy acutely, despite theoretical concerns about reduced musculotendinous stiffness.
No specific type of stretching — static, dynamic, or proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation (PNF) — was found to acutely affect running economy differently from the others.
The common recommendation to avoid pre-running stretching to protect running economy is not supported by current pooled evidence from 15 studies and 181 participants.

Energy storage in tendons, which depends on musculotendinous stiffness, is theoretically important for running economy, but stretching-induced stiffness reductions have not been shown to translate into measurable running economy impairments.
Evidence on chronic (long-term) stretching effects on running economy is extremely limited, with only 2 studies identified, insufficient for meta-analysis.
Methodological quality of studies on stretching and running economy is generally fair, with a mean PEDro score of 4.88 out of 10, limiting certainty of conclusions.
